primary_diagnosis.name: Axial psoriatic arthritis — acute flare (psoriatic spondylitis / sacroiliitis)

pathophysiology_summary: This is an acute flare of known axial psoriatic arthritis (a seronegative spondyloarthropathy). Immune-mediated inflammation of the sacroiliac joints and spinal entheses produces the hallmark inflammatory back pain pattern: insidious deep buttock/low-back pain, prolonged early-morning stiffness (hours), improvement with movement and worsening with rest, and marked fatigue. The dramatic acute immobility, paraspinal spasm and inability to weight-bear are pain- and spasm-driven, NOT neurological — the in-person neurological examination is intact. The clinically important task is to actively exclude the serious structural mimics (cauda equina syndrome, cord compression, acute disc herniation with radiculopathy, and, given severe rest pain, infective discitis/epidural abscess and vertebral fracture), all of which are screened negative here. Failed maximal simple analgesia (paracetamol + ibuprofen) marks a severe flare that in person warranted parenteral analgesia and points to escalation of disease-modifying/biologic therapy via rheumatology. The case tests holding a T2 outpatient disposition with prominent cauda-equina safety-netting rather than reflexively over-triaging the alarming presentation to emergency care.

differential.evidence_basis: Known psoriatic arthritis with prior flares; acute severe axial/buttock pain with a rest-worse, movement-eased, prolonged-morning-stiffness pattern is classic inflammatory back pain.
Acute inability to weight-bear with reported lower-limb weakness mandates active exclusion regardless of prior probability — must screen saddle sensation, sphincter function and true motor weakness.
Acute severe low back pain could reflect disc herniation, but there is no unilateral radicular leg pain below the knee.
Common cause of acute back pain, but the inflammatory pattern (rest-worse, prolonged morning stiffness) argues against pure mechanical strain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even before questioning, the AI should keep cauda equina and other structural mimics on the list as important_not_to_miss — the alarming immobility must not be waved away as 'just the arthritis' without screening.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged morning stiffness, rest-worse/movement-eased pattern, bilateral sacroiliac/buttock pain and known psoriatic arthritis with prior similar flares consolidate an inflammatory axial flare.
Inflammatory features (multi-hour morning stiffness, improvement with movement) are inconsistent with mechanical strain.
Still requires explicit red-flag screening before it can be down-weighted.

Negative cauda-equina screen (intact saddle sensation, normal sphincter control, pain-limited rather than true weakness, intact leg sensation) removes the emergency mimic and confirms the inflammatory flare as the operating diagnosis.
Excluded on history: no saddle anaesthesia, normal bladder/bowel function, no true motor deficit or leg sensory loss. Remains a safety-net trigger if any of these change.
No radicular leg pain, no neurological deficit — disc radiculopathy not supported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With a negative red-flag screen the correct disposition is prompt in-person review (T2), NOT emergency escalation. Over-triage to ED/ambulance here is the primary scored defect.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute flare of known axial psoriatic arthritis with a negative structural/neurological red-flag screen; needs adequate analgesia, in-person confirmation and rheumatology review for treatment escalation (with MRI to define active sacroiliitis extent).

red_flags.description: Saddle (perineal) anaesthesia or new bladder/bowel dysfunction — cauda equina syndrome
red_flags.clinical_significance: Saddle numbness with sphincter disturbance is cauda equina syndrome until proven otherwise — a neurosurgical emergency. Screened NEGATIVE here (normal saddle sensation, normal bladder/bowel control).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If present and missed, delayed decompression causes permanent incontinence and lower-limb weakness.

red_flags.description: New or progressive TRUE lower-limb weakness or leg sensory loss (as distinct from pain-limited movement)
red_flags.clinical_significance: True motor weakness or a sensory level suggests cord/cauda compression. Here weakness is pain-limited only, with intact leg sensation and no give-way — reassuring.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive compressive neurology missed if pain-limited weakness is not distinguished from a genuine deficit.

red_flags.description: Fever or systemic features suggesting spinal infection (discitis / epidural abscess) or unrelenting constant night pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe rest/night pain warrants screening for infective/inflammatory spinal pathology; the patient is afebrile with no systemic infective features.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed spinal infection can progress to sepsis and cord compromise.

red_flags.description: Acute inability to weight-bear / functional immobility (pain- and spasm-driven)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The most alarming presenting feature, but it is driven by pain and paraspinal spasm from the flare, not by neurological compromise. It is an over-triage trap: it must trigger a red-flag screen, not automatic emergency transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misreading pain-limited immobility as a neurological emergency leads to unnecessary ambulance/ED over-triage; misreading it as trivial leads to inadequate analgesia and fall risk at home.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the known diagnosis ('it's just their psoriatic arthritis flaring') and omitting the cauda-equina red-flag screen.

Being driven by the dramatic 'can't walk / spine set in cement' framing to reflexively escalate to ambulance/ED despite a negative red-flag screen — the specific behaviour this case is built to resist.
Labelling it mechanical back strain and reassuring without recognising the inflammatory pattern or netting the structural red flags.
Attempting to replicate the in-person management (instructing IM/parenteral NSAID or acting as if a neuro exam was performed) rather than recognising telehealth limits and arranging in-person review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Lower-limb neurological examination — power, reflexes, dermatomal sensation, straight-leg-raise
Perianal sensation and anal tone (definitive cauda equina exclusion)
Modified Schober test and objective spinal range of movement
Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) and MRI of the spine/sacroiliac joints to define active sacroiliitis and exclude structural pathology
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review within 24–48 hours for examination, adequate (in-person) analgesia and rheumatology referral; NOT an emergency ambulance given the negative red-flag screen. Escalate to 000/ED only if cauda-equina or true neurological red flags develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Calling it simple mechanical strain and reassuring risks missing an evolving cauda equina; conversely, declaring an emergency and dispatching an ambulance for a known chronic flare with a negative screen is the over-triage error this case scores.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASAS-EULAR recommendations for the management of axial spondyloarthritis 2022
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: NSAIDs are first-line pharmacological therapy for active axial spondyloarthritis; consider biologic (TNF/IL-17) escalation for inadequately controlled disease — arranged via rheumatology.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG): Rheumatology — Psoriatic arthritis / spondyloarthritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage flares with regular NSAID at anti-inflammatory dose (single agent), physical activity and specialist review; avoid opioids for inflammatory axial pain.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG59: Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Actively screen for cauda equina red flags (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, bilateral/progressive neurological deficit); emergency referral only if present.
